turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court in case 24-354, The Federal Communications Commission versus Consumers' Research, and the consolidated case.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Nearly a century ago, Congress charged the Federal Communications Commission, for short, the FCC, with making communications services available at affordable prices for all Americans. That objective is known as universal service. The idea behind it is this. Basic communications services are and long have been essential to participation in modern life, but ordinary market mechanisms are prone to leave some segments of the population, particularly those in rural and low income areas, without adequate access to those services. So there is a role for legislation to bridge the gap. In 1996, Congress enacted a statute that provides the FCC new and more detailed instructions about how to pursue universal service. The statute directs the FCC to collect money from telecommunications companies, often called carriers, and to use that money to subsidize basic communications services for underserved communities. The statute calls the monetary charges imposed on the carriers contributions, although they are in fact mandatory. In this case, a nonprofit organization called Consumers' Research challenges the universal service program, saying in particular that the contribution scheme, which funds everything in the program, violates the Constitution. The Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit agreed that the scheme was unlawful. Today, we reject the Consumers' Research challenge, reverse the Fifth Circuit, and uphold the universal service contribution scheme. The primary claim Consumers' Research makes is that Congress violated what's called the non-delegation doctrine when it authorized the FCC to collect contributions from carriers. Here's some background about what the non-delegation doctrine is. The Constitution vests all legislative power in Congress and Congress cannot give it away, cannot delegate its legislative power to anyone else. That's a core principle. But Congress may, in fact, really has to, seek assistance from the Executive Branch from agencies like the FCC to carry out many of the laws it enacts. And in authorizing agencies to fulfill their statutory missions, Congress can give them, and again, often has to give them, a measure of discretion. How much discretion? The tests we've long employed to separate the permissible from the impermissible is called the Intelligible Principle Test. If a law contains an intelligible principle to guide the agency as it implements a legislative scheme, it complies with the Constitution. Even so, Consumers' Research argues that we should apply a different test here. That's because it says universal service contributions are taxes, and tax statutes, Consumers' Research contends, comport with the non-delegation doctrine only if they contain a numeric limit on how much revenue may be raised, a specific dollar cap or a fixed tax rate. Everyone agrees that the universal service statute doesn't have either one of those things. So, according to Consumers' Research, it violates the non-delegation doctrine even if it has a non-numeric intelligible principle to guide the agency's discretion. We reject that argument today in large part because we've rejected it twice before. In two prior cases, litigants asked the Court to hold that a stricter non-delegation rule applies to statutes based on Congress' taxing power. Each time, we refused, holding that the usual intelligible principle test applies. Today, we say so again. Revenue-raising statutes are no different from others. If they give adequate guidance to the executive in exercising its discretion, they do not offend the non-delegation doctrine. And we go on to hold the universal service contribution scheme satisfies that usual test. The statute tells the FCC to raise enough money, the term in the statute is sufficient contributions, to fund universal service subsidy programs, no more and no less. And the statute provides clear instructions about the content of those programs. Universal service subsidies must serve specific beneficiaries, low-income consumers, those in rural areas, rural hospitals in particular, and schools and libraries. And those subsidies may go only towards specific kinds of communications services, those which are widely used, generally affordable, and essential for modern life. Those criteria put bounds on the universal service program and they give the FCC intelligible principles to follow in carrying out Congress' instructions. So the universal service contribution scheme does not violate the non-delegation doctrine. A separate claim Consumers' Research brings is that the FCC has impermissibly given away its power over universal service contributions to a private company. That claim invokes a different set of our precedents which hold that while agencies like the FCC can obtain assistance from private actors, they cannot give private actors the ability to exercise government power themselves. Sometimes that principle is known as the private non-delegation doctrine. The private company whose role Consumers' Research objects to is called the Universal Service Administrative Company. It is a nonprofit corporation owned by an association of carriers, and the FCC has enlisted it to help administer its universal service subsidy programs. Among other things, the company is involved in the process of determining the amount of contributions that each of the carriers must pay. Consumers' Research says it is too much involved, that the FCC has given the company a free hand to set the amount of contributions. The Consumers' Research is incorrect. The FCC, not the company, decides how much carriers must contribute. All the company does is produce financial projections for the FCC's review, revision, and approval. Those projections, if accepted, are indeed critical in setting the amount of contributions that must be paid, but they are recommendations only. They have no legal or practical effect unless and until the FCC decides they should. So the company exercises no governmental power and the FCC's use of it poses no constitutional problem. The final question this case raises is whether the combination of Congress' grant of authority to the FCC and the FCC's use of a private company violates the Constitution even if neither one does so alone. That is what the Fifth Circuit held, but it was wrong. Congress' lawful conferral of discretionary authority on the FCC, plus the FCC's lawful conferral of advisory responsibilities on a private party does not somehow equal an unlawful combination. Rather, it equals a permissible arrangement under our Constitution. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Jackson has also filed a concurring opinion. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas and Alito join.
